source_paper_title: Counting and Enumerating Optimum Cut Sets for Hypergraph k-Partitioning Problems for Fixed k
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Beideman_2024, title={Counting and Enumerating Optimum Cut Sets for Hypergraph k-Partitioning Problems for Fixed k}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0259}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0259}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Beideman, Calvin and Chandrasekaran, Karthekeyan and Wang, Weihang}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2579–2601} }
context:
Page 24, Section 7 (Conclusion)

Resolving the complexity of this variant of the hypergraph k-partitioning problem for \(k \ge 5\) remains open.
title: Determine complexity of minimizing sum of boundary cut sizes in hypergraph k-partitions
background_latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a (multi-)hypergraph with vertex set \(V\) of size \(n\) and hyperedge set \(E\). Each hyperedge \(e\in E\) is a subset of \(V\). (The paper primarily discusses the unweighted case, i.e., each hyperedge has unit cost; the objective below is stated for this setting.)

For a nonempty proper subset \(U\subset V\), define the cut-set
\[\delta(U):=\{e\in E: e\cap U\neq\emptyset \text{ and } e\cap (V\setminus U)\neq\emptyset\},\]
and its cut value \(d(U):=|\delta(U)|\).

A \(k\)-partition of \(V\) is an ordered tuple \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\) of pairwise disjoint nonempty subsets whose union is \(V\).

For a \(k\)-partition \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\), the paper considers various objectives based on the boundary sizes \(|\delta(V_i)|\). In particular, the variant highlighted in the conclusion asks to minimize the sum of boundary cut values
\[\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| = \sum_{i=1}^k d(V_i).\]
problem_definition_latex:
Given a hypergraph \(G=(V,E)\) and a fixed integer \(k\ge 5\), determine the computational complexity of the optimization problem
\[\min\left\{\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| : (V_1,\dots,V_k) \text{ is a } k\text{-partition of } V\right\}.\]
In particular, decide whether this problem admits a polynomial-time algorithm for fixed \(k\ge 5\) (or whether it is NP-hard) in the unweighted hypergraph setting.
latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a (multi-)hypergraph with vertex set \(V\) of size \(n\) and hyperedge set \(E\). Each hyperedge \(e\in E\) is a subset of \(V\). (The paper primarily discusses the unweighted case, i.e., each hyperedge has unit cost; the objective below is stated for this setting.)

For a nonempty proper subset \(U\subset V\), define the cut-set
\[\delta(U):=\{e\in E: e\cap U\neq\emptyset \text{ and } e\cap (V\setminus U)\neq\emptyset\},\]
and its cut value \(d(U):=|\delta(U)|\).

A \(k\)-partition of \(V\) is an ordered tuple \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\) of pairwise disjoint nonempty subsets whose union is \(V\).

For a \(k\)-partition \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\), the paper considers various objectives based on the boundary sizes \(|\delta(V_i)|\). In particular, the variant highlighted in the conclusion asks to minimize the sum of boundary cut values
\[\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| = \sum_{i=1}^k d(V_i).\]

Given a hypergraph \(G=(V,E)\) and a fixed integer \(k\ge 5\), determine the computational complexity of the optimization problem
\[\min\left\{\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| : (V_1,\dots,V_k) \text{ is a } k\text{-partition of } V\right\}.\]
In particular, decide whether this problem admits a polynomial-time algorithm for fixed \(k\ge 5\) (or whether it is NP-hard) in the unweighted hypergraph setting.
open_problem_quote: Resolving the complexity of this variant of the hypergraph k-partitioning problem for \(k \ge 5\) remains open.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is a complexity-status question (polynomial-time solvable vs NP-hard) rather than a universal inequality that can be refuted by a finite instance search. While one can experiment with heuristics or reductions, automated counterexample search is not a faithful route to resolving the stated problem.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could explore algorithmic ideas (e.g., dynamic programming for fixed \(k\), relaxations, or exact solvers on structured instances) and test performance, but empirical success would not establish polynomial-time solvability or hardness. It may still help by discovering patterns or candidate reductions/algorithms that can later be proved.
